title: Cat Trembling All Over: Causes and Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is trembling continuously, it may be a high-priority emergency. Learn why whole-body tremors happen and when to seek immediate vet care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Continuous whole-body trembling in cats is a high-priority emergency typically caused by toxins or neurological issues and requires immediate veterinary care. A short video of the behavior can help your veterinarian quickly differentiate between tremors, seizures, and pain.

What does it mean if my cat has whole-body trembling?

Continuous trembling, or generalized tremors, involves involuntary, rhythmic muscle contractions that affect your cat's entire body. Unlike normal shivering from cold or excitement, these tremors are persistent and often indicate an underlying disruption in the nervous system, a metabolic imbalance, or the presence of a toxin.

Is my cat's continuous trembling a medical emergency?

Yes, the urgency level for continuous whole-body trembling is High. Immediate veterinary intervention is critical because:

  • Trembling is frequently a sign of acute poisoning (such as permethrin or lily toxicity), severe electrolyte imbalances, or neurological distress.
  • If your cat is trembling non-stop, you must contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.
  • Untreated tremors can lead to hyperthermia, seizures, or permanent organ damage.

Why should I take a video of my cat's trembling for the veterinary team?

While it may feel counterintuitive to record your cat during a crisis, a 10-second video is incredibly valuable for triage and diagnosis for several reasons:

  • Cats often hide symptoms or change their behavior once they arrive at a clinic due to adrenaline.
  • A video allows the vet to see the exact frequency and intensity of the tremors as they occur at home.
  • It helps the veterinary team differentiate between muscle tremors, seizures, or pain-related shivering for a much faster diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In emergency veterinary triage, generalized trembling in cats necessitates prompt assessment. Potential causes include toxicities such as chlorinated hydrocarbon exposure, where trembling or shivering may precede convulsions. Diagnostic considerations should also include hypocalcemia, organophosphate toxicity, thiamine deficiency, and sepsis. Treatment should be directed at the underlying cause, and may include calcium gluconate for hypocalcemia, atropine for organophosphate toxicity, thiamine supplementation, and antibiotics if sepsis is suspected. In cases of trauma, treatment should minimize blood loss, provide fluid therapy and respiratory support, and reduce pain.

Chapter: Emergency, Toxicology, Surgery, Cardiology, Pulmonology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1660)

faqs.question: Can a cat tremble just from being cold?
faqs.answer: While cats can shiver if they are cold, this usually stops once they are warmed. Continuous, non-stop trembling that does not resolve with warmth is typically a sign of a medical emergency.

faqs.question: What toxins cause full-body tremors in cats?
faqs.answer: Common causes include permethrin (found in some dog flea treatments), moldy food, certain houseplants, and slug bait (metaldehyde).

faqs.question: Should I try to give my cat water if they are trembling?
faqs.answer: No. If your cat is trembling uncontrollably, they may have difficulty swallowing or could choke. Focus on getting them to an emergency vet as quickly as possible.
